coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-off sensing of lead and gentamicin based on phosphorus and chlorine co-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sefid-Sefidehkhan, Yasaman
Jouyban, Abolghasem
Rahimpour, Elaheh
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, CQDs co-doped with phosphorus and chlorine have been synthesized by a hydrothermal technique. The synthesized CQDs possess a mean size of <20 nm and show excitation-independent features. The investigations presented that the CQD’s fluorescence intensity was markedly quenched in the presence Pb2+ due to the formation of a stable non-luminescent complex that regarding this mechanism, a fluorescent platform was offered for Pb2+ ions in water samples. Furthermore, the gentamicin adding into the P, Cl-CQDs + Pb system had a more quenching effect on CQD’s fluorescence and so another probe was proposed for gentamicin determination. The detection limits for the proposed methods were 0.001 µg.mL−1 for Pb2+and 0.003 µg.mL−1 for gentamicin, respectively. The probe was successfully utilized in the environmental samples with high accuracy and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Probe
Lead
Gentamicin
Carbon dots
Central composite design

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of P, Cl co-doped CQDs
Analysis of Pb2+and gentamicin on real samples
Utilizing the developed probes for real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Affairs of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Research Affairs of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, under grant number 69783 . The authors would also like to give special thanks to Mr. Habib Rahnema for collecting the real samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image; and (B) ATR-FTIR of P,Cl-CQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the P, Cl-CQDs in the absence (a) and presence of Pb2+ ions in different concentrations b-g (0.006–1.8 µg mL−1); (B) Calibration curve for Pb2+ quantification. Conditions: pH 4.0, and CQD’s amount of 65 µL and time = 60 min. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the P, Cl-CQDs + Pb system in the absence (a) and presence of gentamicin in different concentrations b-g (0.01–3.0 µg mL−1); (D) Calibration curve for gentamicin quantification. Conditions: pH 4.0, and CQD’s amount of 65 µL, Pb2+ concentration = 0.6 µg mL−1 and time = immediately.
(A) The impact of time on the response signal; (B) Counter plots obtained based on the quadratic model of F0-F against the investigated parameters.

(A) Selectivity of P, Cl-CQDs toward Pb2+ in the presence of other metal ions with a concentration two times higher than Pb2+; (B) Selectivity of P, Cl-CQDs + Pb system toward gentamicin in the presence of other antibiotics with a concentration two times higher than gentamicin.
Schematic representation of synthesis procedures of P,Cl-CQDs and their response to Pb2+ ions and gentamicin.
Comparison of figures of merit of the validated method with other methods for quantification of investigated analytes.

Metal organic framework 199-derived highly porous copper nano-foam fiber

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Pb2+ and gentamicin in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-off sensing of lead and gentamicin based on phosphorus and chlorine co-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, CQDs co-doped with phosphorus and chlorine have been synthesized by a hydrothermal technique. The synthesized CQDs possess a mean size of <20 nm and show excitation-independent features. The investigations presented that the CQD’s fluorescence intensity was markedly quenched in the presence Pb2+ due to the formation of a stable non-luminescent complex that regarding this mechanism, a fluorescent platform was offered for Pb2+ ions in water samples. Furthermore, the gentamicin adding into the P, Cl-CQDs + Pb system had a more quenching effect on CQD’s fluorescence and so another probe was proposed for gentamicin determination. The detection limits for the proposed methods were 0.001 µg.mL−1 for Pb2+and 0.003 µg.mL−1 for gentamicin, respectively. The probe was successfully utilized in the environmental samples with high accuracy and selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are fluorescent materials in nano size owing to their high quantum confinement impact with adjustable optoelectronic and photo-luminescent features [1]. Inertness, photo-induced electron transfer, high biocompatibility, tunable luminescence, and little toxicity behavior are the unique properties of CQDs. The original CQDs are composed of carbon atoms with sp2 and sp3 hybridized orbitals containing carbon (C), oxygen (O) and hydrogen (H) atoms [2]. The functionalization is a known way to modify their photo-luminescence properties, unparticular for enhancing their efficacy for detection purposes. modification methods usually are entering functional groups e.g. amine [3], hydroxyl [4,5] and carboxyl groups [6]. The QDs modification not only leads to surface defects responsible for fluorescence origination but also enhances the active sites for analyte binding. Another way for CQD’s modification is doping with heteroatoms, which is performed using the replacement of components of the CQDs with guest atoms with various valances. Two kinds of doping processes are recognized, including n-type and p-type doping. N-type doping is used as an electron donating portion, e.g., N-, S-, P-, and Cl-doping [7-9]. Whereas p-type doping is an acceptor electron portion, e.g., B-doping [10]. Doping with heteroatoms is a reliable method to adjust the fluorescent features for sensing purposes in the various fields of medical, pharmaceutical, industrial and environmental applications.

The main focus of the current work is on environmental pollution. Heavy metal pollution as a significant class of contaminants possesses a harmful impact on human health, even at trace amounts [11]. Owing to the unique fluorescence features, CQDs have been introduced as efficient materials for heavy metal analysis [12]. Lead (Pb2+) is a heavy metal ion that has a large number of harmful effects on ecological patterns, the human and environmental health. In concentrations higher than 5 μmol.L−1 in blood, Pb2+ leads to developmental disabilities, hypertension, renal impairment, anemia, abnormalities in the nervous/reproductive system, and dysfunction. So, USEPA reported the maximum acceptable amount of 0.07 μg.L−1 for Pb2+ in drinking water [13]. So far, various methods have been employed for the determination of Pb2+ in different matrices including electrochemical methods [14,15], ICP-OES [16], graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry [17,18], thermal lens spectroscopy [19], tungsten electrothermal vaporization-flame atomic fluorescence spectrometry [20], and spectrophotometric [21]. However, there are some limited works reported on Pb2+ determination with fluorescent nanoparticles probes such as CQDs [22,23] and N-CQDs [24], N-CQDs conjugated with Fe3O4 [25] and B-CQDs [26]. Herein, we used a P, Cl co-doped CQD for Pb2+ concentration monitoring in the water samples.

On the other hand, since several antibiotics are known to disrupt plant protein synthesis, the inherent toxicity of the antibiotics can also be a serious environmental hazard. Antibiotics are widely used in human and veterinary medicine to combat bacteria. Gentamicin is an aminoglycoside that belongs to the class of bactericidal antibiotics that have an effect against infections related to gram-negative bacteria. Despite prompting nephrotoxicity, gentamicin is widely utilized because of its high activity against infections of gram-negative bacteria raised by Proteus, Serratia, and Pseudomonas. Gentamicin-nephrotoxicity includes generation of renal free radical, decreased antioxidant defense mechanisms, congestion glomerular and acute tubular necrosis, causing the reduced rate of glomerular filtration and kidney dysfunction. Therefore, detection of residual antibiotics in meat, dairy, and other food products is imperative [27] and is mainly backed by the water that they are fed. In the second part of the current work, the P, Cl-co doped + Pb system is set for the quantification and monitoring of gentamicin in the environmental samples. Although, various methods were reported for the determination of gentamicin in the different matrices such as electrochemical [28], colorimetry [29], ion-pair chromatography coupled with evaporative light scattering detection [30], and HPLC -laser-based polarimetric detection [31], development a sensitive and reliable method are always highly demanded by researchers and clinicians.

Maltose (C12H22O11·H2O, 97.0%), O-phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85.0%), lead (II) acetate tri-hydrate (Pb(CH3COO)2·3H2O, 99.5%), bismuth (III) nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O,98%), strontium nitrate (Sr(NO3)2, 99.0%), aluminium chloride (AlCl3·6H2O, 97.0%), nickel chloride (NiCl2·6H2O, 98.0%), manganese (II) chloride (MnCl2·4H2O, 99.0%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.9%), zinc chloride (ZnCl, 98–100.5%), mercuric nitrate (Hg(NO3)2, 99.0%) and iron (III) chloride (FeCl3·6H2O, 99.0%) from Merck, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37.0%), copper (II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 99.0–104%) from Sigma, silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9%), cadmium chloride (CdCl2, 99.0%), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4·7H2O, 99.0%) from Panreac, cobalt (II) chloride (CoCl2, 99.0%) from scharlau are the used materials in the current work. All chemicals were of analytical grade. The stock solution of 1000 mg.L−1 of each analyte was prepared, and the Pb2+ and gentamicin working solutions were obtained by diluting the stock solution.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) schemes were obtained utilizing a CM30 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Philips, The Netherlands, https://www.philips.com). Attenuated total reflection infrared (ATR-IR) spectrum was performed using a Perkin Elmer (USA, www.perkinelmer.com) ATR-IR spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured using a Jasco FP-750 fluorescence spectrometer. Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclaves and an electrothermal blast oven (Behdad, Tehran, Iran) were employed to synthesize the P, Cl co-doped CQDs. An analytical balance (model AB204-S, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) and a digital pH-meter (model 744, Metrohm Ltd., Switzerland) are other instruments employed in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of P, Cl co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The P, Cl co-doped CQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method for initial reagents of phosphoric acid, maltose, and hydrochloric acid. During the high temperatures in an autoclave [32], P and Cl are replaced by C and H atoms. In detail, 0.5 g of maltose monohydrate, 0.15 mL of phosphoric acid, and 0.15 mL of hydrochloric acid were placed in 20 mL of deionized water in a vessel. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a drying oven. During the heating, the mixture changed from colorless to a turbid yellow. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to separate larger suspended particles.

For Pb2+ detection, 65 µL of P, Cl co-doped CQDs solution was added to 100 µL acetate buffer (pH = 4.0, 1.0 mmol.L−1) and then shaken well, and various amounts of Pb2+ standard solution were added to the mixture. Afterward, the mixture was diluted with deionized water to 1.0 mL and incubated for 60 min. The solution was transferred to a fluorescence cell and the fluorescence intensity was measured at λem = 480 nm with an excitation wavelength at λex = 390 nm with slit widths of 10.0 and 10.0 nm for emission and excitation, respectively.

For gentamicin detection, CQDs in the presence of Pb2+ act as a system in which their variation in the presence of gentamicin can be quantified as a response toward gentamicin. Briefly, 65 µL of P, Cl co-doped CQDs and 100 µL acetate buffer (pH = 4, 1.0 mmol.L−1) were added to a micro-tube with 0.6 µg.mL−1 Pb2+. Then various amounts of gentamicin were added to the mixture and it reached a volume of 1.0 mL. The fluorescence spectra for the above solution were recorded at the previously mentioned condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Pb2+and gentamicin on real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water and wastewater samples were employed to study the practical applicability of the platforms on real samples. The samples were sampled from the Mehran River (Tabriz, Iran), spring water (Sis, Iran), spring water (Jazireh Islami, Iran), spring water ( Shabestar, Iran), and tap water was sampled from the local drinking water (Tabriz) and utilized for analysis directly. The content of Pb2+ and gentamicin in the real samples was quantified by the abovementioned method.

In order to find the size and morphology of nanoparticles, the TEM image of P, Cl-CQDs was provided, and as can be seen from Fig. 1 A, nanoparticles show a uniform shape with a mean size of <20 nm. Furthermore, to investigate the surface functionalized groups, ATR-FTIR was performed. Fig. 1B shows the ATR-FTIR spectra of synthesized nanoparticles, which present a broad absorption peak in 3085 to 3750 cm−1 (νs of –OH), indicating the hydroxyl groups that cause high hydrophilicity. Furthermore, the bands at 1705 (νs of C = O), 1560 (νs of C = C), and 1220 cm−1 (νs of C–O) show that the P, Cl-CQDs possess carboxylic acid groups that more support their dispersibility in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the new absorption bands at 1002 (νs of P–O) and 682 cm−1 (νs of C–Cl) demonstrate the efficient doping of CQDs with P and Cl atoms.

The maximum emission intensity for P, Cl-CQDs was recorded at 480 nm with an excitation wavelength of 390 nm (Fig. 1S (A)). The fluorescence intensity of P, Cl-CQDs was gradually quenched when Pb2+ ion in the concentration range of 0.006–1.8 µg.mL−1 was increased in optimized reaction conditions (Fig. 2 A). The observed quenching can be related to the formation of a non-luminescent stable complex between P, Cl-CQDs and Pb2+ ions due to the high affinity of phosphorous and oxygen groups to Pb2+ ions. There are several reports in the literature on high coordination affinity between the Pb2+ and phosphorous and oxygen groups that supply this mechanism [33-35]. The chelating of Pb2+ ions with P, Cl-CQDs gets them into proximity with each other. A non-radiative electron-transfer was performed from the CQD’s excited states to the ‘d’ orbital of Pb2+ ions and its fluorescence was strongly quenched [36]. So based on this mechanism, a “turn-off” probe was validated for the quantification of Pb2+ ions in the water samples.

In the next step of our studies, we find that gentamicin adding to the P, Cl-CQDs + Pb system had a more quenching impact on CD’s fluorescence which is proportional to gentamicin concentration increasing in the concentration range of 0.01–3.0 µg.mL−1 (Fig. 2A). It should be said that direct determination of gentamicin with P, Cl-CQDs produces a weak signal response for the used concentration (Fig. 1S (B)). The complexation of various antibiotics with metal ions has previously been studied completely [37]. Observed quenching for QD’s + metal ion system after complexation with a ligand (especially pharmaceutical compound) had been reported in a work for determination of penicillamine as a copper chelator by Cu-graphene QDs [38] and deferiprone as an iron chelator by graphene QDs [39]. A schematic representation of the response of a system to Pb2+ and gentamicin is given in Scheme 1 .

The effects of important parameters on the sensing performance of two proposed sensors were investigated by central composite design (CCD) to achieve optimum conditions. To check the reliability of the used model, the parameter’s effect and their interactions, ANOVA analysis was used. The validity of the prediction model was evaluated using R2 and adjusted- R2 values. The main parameters such as pH, buffer concentration and NPs volume were investigated by CCD and reaction time was investigated using the one-at-a-time technique. 60 min was the required time for completion of the reaction between Pb2+ and P, Cl-CQDs (Fig. 3 A). Counter plots were used to show the individual and interaction impact of selected parameters on the response. As you see in Fig. 3B, the optimal parameters of Pb sensing procedure are pH 4.0, and a buffer concentration of 0.1 mmol.L−1, CQD’s amount of 65 µL. Investigation ranges are pH of 2.0–6.0, buffer concentration of 0.01–0.4 mmol.L−1 and CQD’s amount of 5.0–85.0 µL. The results of ANOVA calculation are given in Table 1S. All the linear and quadratic model terms, and the one interaction term (X13) are significant. Herein, R2 is 0.9447 and adjusted R2 is 0.9155 which show a high correlation between experimental and predicted results. According to ANOVA data, F value is 32.29 and P value is <0.0001 which confirm that the model is reliable for prediction. The P value of lack-of-fit (0.4367) is not significant and confirms the model’s accuracy. A second-order polynomial model was defined based on the obtained data, which in the coded factors is as:
(1)
Y = 1.67386 + 0.0703143
X 1
- 0.0508597
X 2
+ 0.231041
X 3
- 0.0967332
X 13
-
0.0620463
X 11
- 0.052243
X 22
- 0.0706035
X 33

Where Y is the fluorescence of QDs in the absence and presence of Pb2+ (F0-F), and X1, X2, and X3 are the pH, buffer concentration, and QD volume, respectively. In addition, the above equation in the actual factors is as follows:
(2)
Y = - 1.48307 + 0.784335
X 1
+ 0.00731361
X 2
+ 0.0467845
X 3
- 0.00483666
X 13
- 0.0620463
X 11
- 9.06997 e - 05
X 22
- 0.000176509
X 33

Fig. 2S (A) gives a comparison of experimental and predicted F0-F of Pb2+ and Fig. 2S (B) summarizes the correlation between the internally studentized residuals and the normal probability (%). The high linearity shows that there was no apparent problem with normality and no signal transformation was needed.

These obtained optimal points (except reaction time) are used in the gentamicin sensing method. The experiments show that gentamicin can affect the P, Cl-CQDs + Pb system signals in the initial time with a reasonable difference. So, the determination was performed immediately after sample preparation. This can be related to the good interaction of gentamicin with Pb and P, Cl-CQDs.

The analytical figure of merits for quantification of Pb2+ and gentamicin in the water samples was investigated by investigating the calibration range, detection limit (LOD), precision, accuracy, and selectivity at optimum reaction conditions. The calibration plot was drawn by taking the fluorescence intensity difference value at 480 nm against concentrations of Pb2+ and gentamicin, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 3B and D, a good linear relationship was shown in a concentration range of 0.006–1.8 µg.mL−1 with a coefficient of determination of 0.9975 for Pb2+sensing and in the concentration range of 0.01–3.0 µg.mL−1 with a coefficient of determination of 0.9959 for gentamicin sensing. The LOD for the Pb2+ and gentamicin quantification in water samples was 0.001 µg.mL−1 and 0.003 µg.mL−1 by using 3 Sb/m (in which Sb: blank’s standard deviation; m: calibration slope). Moreover, the method precision for Pb2+and gentamicin determination was obtained by employing relative standard deviations (RSDs %) of replicated determinations of 0.3 µg.mL−1 of Pb2+ and 0.5 µg.mL−1 on the same day and different days. The inter -day and intra-day RSDs% were 0.8 % and 1.1%, respectively for Pb2+analysis and 0.9 % and 1.7 %, respectively for gentamicin analysis. The developed methods for the determination of Pb2+ and gentamicin were also compared with reported techniques and summarized in Table 1 . According to the analytical characterization reported for each method, the proposed methods in the current work are good and comparable with other reported methods for each analyte. Although all the quantification methods have their particular strengths and weaknesses, if we compare them, it can be concluded that separation methods such as HPLC and CE methods are more time-consuming and need a skilled operator and an extra step for sample preparation prior to injecting the sample into the system. Furthermore, the main disadvantage of electrochemical methods is their low repeatability and reproducibility, which limits their use. The optical methods are simple, low-cost, and portable (in most cases). However, the main limitations of these methods include low sensitivity, and low specificity. Recent researches have focused on developing innovative nanomaterials and controling their properties to meet the needs of specific applications. The power and scope of such nanomaterials can be improved by coupling with other analytical methods. Herein, we employed a doped nanoparticle increase the selectivity of our systems toward the chosen analytes, which was not reported previously in the literature, and as can be seen, its figures of merits are comparable with highly accurate methods like HPLC. These methods have the potential for on-field measurements.

To study the selectivity of the validated methods toward Pb2+ and gentamicin, analysis was performed in the presence of other metal ions for Pb2+ analysis and other antibiotics in the optimized condition. For this purpose, the effect of each compound and specie with a concentration two times higher than the desired analytes was investigated on the response signal of the system, and the variation in fluorescence intensity was recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 4 A and B, the studied species show no significant effect on the response. These results demonstrate that the developed methods for Pb2+ and gentamicin have satisfactory selectivity for their determination in the water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilizing the developed probes for real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the utility of the developed probes, their application for Pb2+ and gentamicin sensing in some environmental water and wastewater samples were also studied and responses were reported in Table 2 . To examine the reliability of the proposed methods, different amounts of the Pb2+ and gentamicin were spiked into the investigated samples and analyzed at optimum conditions as given in Table 2. As can be seen, the recoveries of spiked samples were in the satisfactory and reasonable ranges (91.6–107.0%), which indicates the capability of these systems for the quantification of the investigated analytes in environmental samples.

In the current work, P-Cl co-doped CQDs with strong fluorescence were prepared using a simple, and cost-effective hydrothermal method with maltose, phosphoric acid, and hydrochloric acid. The fluorescent intensity of prepared CQDs is quenched with Pb2+ and gentamicin in different conditions. The probes have been successfully validated and applied for the quantification of Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0.006–1.8 µg.mL−1 and gentamicin in the concentration range of 0.01–3.0 µg.mL−1 in water and wastewater samples. Validated methods have demonstrated several advantages, including low LOD, few interfering substances, low cost, acceptable selectivity and sensitivity for quantification of Pb2+ and gentamicin in the water sample that will provide a promising vision in environmental and other areas in the future.
Yasaman Sefid-Sefidehkhan: Investigation. Abolghasem Jouyban: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Elaheh Rahimpour: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Research Affairs of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, under grant number 69783. The authors would also like to give special thanks to Mr. Habib Rahnema for collecting the real samples.

Mater. Technol.

J. Mater. Chem. B.
